title: Hubbard Hall — constituent analysis
text:
1 Hubbard Hall constituent analysis
This book explores Hubbard Hall’s constituents — donors, patrons, and class-takers — using data pulled directly from the Neon CRM API. Every figure is reproducible: data is extracted, cleaned, geocoded, and summarized by the hh package, then read from saved parquet tables in data/20_processed/. All numbers in the prose below are computed from the data, so they stay correct as the data is refreshed.
All charts here are aggregates (counts and dollar totals by category, distance band, and day of week) — no individual is identified.
Headline findings


Code
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from hh.io import read_parquet
from hh.viz import style, CATEGORICAL, BLUE, SEQUENTIAL_BLUE, INK, MUTED

households = read_parquet("processed", "households_summary.parquet")
regs = read_parquet("processed", "registrations_enriched.parquet")

# headline figures (computed, not hardcoded)
_order = ["[0, 1)", "[1, 5)", "[5, 10)", "[10, 20)", "[20, ∞)"]
_d = (
    households[households["is_donor"]]
    .groupby("distance_band", observed=True)["total_donations"]
    .sum()
    .reindex(_order)
    .fillna(0)
)
_c = _d.cumsum() / _d.sum()
within5, within10, within20 = (
    f"{_c.loc[b]:.0%}" for b in ("[1, 5)", "[5, 10)", "[10, 20)")
)
n_hh = f"{len(households):,}"
n_donors = f"{int(households['is_donor'].sum()):,}"
n_patrons = f"{int(households['is_patron'].sum()):,}"
n_class = f"{int(households['is_class_taker'].sum()):,}"
n_donor_class = f"{int((households['is_donor'] & households['is_class_taker']).sum()):,}"
n_all3 = f"{int((households['is_donor'] & households['is_patron'] & households['is_class_taker']).sum()):,}"



Donors are local. About 54% of donated dollars come from within 10 miles of Hubbard Hall (41% within 5 miles; 77% within 20 miles).
Theater draws on Saturday; music on Sunday. But within a theater run that plays Fri/Sat/Sun, Saturday leads only because it usually has two shows — per performance the three days are essentially tied, and Saturday matinees are the weekend’s weakest slot. The ranking is shifting: since 2021, Sunday has been the best-attended day per performance in nearly every full-weekend run.
Strong overlap: of 4,598 households, 1,298 donate, 2,745 attend performances, and 1,819 take classes; 629 both donate and take classes, 418 do all three.


A note on “multi-day runs.” Each performance night is a separate Neon event — there is no field linking the nights of a run — so runs are reconstructed by manually mapping event names to productions (hh.analytics.productions). Attendance by day within Fri/Sat/Sun theater runs is in the When people come chapter.

title: 2  Where donors live
text:
How far do Hubbard Hall’s donors live from the hall? Distances are straight-line (haversine) from the venue (25 E Main St, Cambridge NY) to each donor household, bucketed into half-open mile bands. Donors whose only address is a PO box are placed at their ZIP’s centroid and flagged “approximate” (excluded from the tight 1-mile count so it stays honest).


Code
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from hh.io import read_parquet
from hh.viz import style, BLUE, money, pct

households = read_parquet("processed", "households_summary.parquet")
donors = households[households["is_donor"]]
by_band = (
    donors.groupby("distance_band", observed=True)
    .agg(
        households=("id", "count"),
        dollars=("total_donations", "sum"),
        mean_gift=("total_donations", "mean"),
    )
    .reset_index()
)
by_band["share"] = by_band["dollars"] / by_band["dollars"].sum()
by_band["cum_share"] = by_band["dollars"].cumsum() / by_band["dollars"].sum()
by_band.style.format(
    {"dollars": money, "mean_gift": money, "share": pct, "cum_share": pct}
)

fig = px.bar(by_band, x="distance_band", y="dollars", color_discrete_sequence=[BLUE])
fig.update_layout(
    showlegend=False,
    xaxis_title="Distance from Hubbard Hall (miles)",
    yaxis_title="Donated $",
)
fig.update_yaxes(tickprefix="$", tickformat=",.0f")
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="$%{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 2.1: Donated dollars by distance band (miles)

fig = px.bar(by_band, x="distance_band", y="households", color_discrete_sequence=[BLUE])
fig.update_layout(
    showlegend=False,
    xaxis_title="Distance from Hubbard Hall (miles)",
    yaxis_title="Donor households",
)
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="%{y:,.0f} households<extra></extra>")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 2.2: Donor households by distance band (miles)






Code
fig = px.bar(by_band, x="distance_band", y="mean_gift", color_discrete_sequence=[BLUE])
fig.update_layout(
    showlegend=False,
    xaxis_title="Distance from Hubbard Hall (miles)",
    yaxis_title="Average donation per household",
)
fig.update_yaxes(tickprefix="$", tickformat=",.0f")
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="$%{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 2.3: Average donation per donor household by distance band (miles)




The tightest band includes some approximate (ZIP-centroid) placements — its centroid share, to read the 1-mile count in context:


Code
prec = (
    donors.groupby("distance_band", observed=True)["geo_precision"]
    .apply(lambda s: (s == "centroid").mean())
    .to_frame("centroid_share")
)
prec.style.format({"centroid_share": pct})

title: 3  When people come
text:
3.1 Within a run: Friday, Saturday, or Sunday?
Which days of the week draw the most attendance? Counts below are registrations (a proxy for headcount), grouped by the event’s performance date. Each panel is one kind of programming.
The same data as a table — each programming type (rows) by day of week (columns), sorted by type then day:
Theater peaks Saturday; music peaks Sunday. Classes spread across the week with a Friday peak.
The next table is about distance, not volume: for theater only, it shows how many attendees came from each distance band on each day of the week (rows = day, columns = distance band). It answers questions like “do Friday/Saturday theater audiences travel farther than Sunday’s?”
The panels above pool every theater event, including one-night shows, so a “Saturday peak” could just mean more shows are booked on Saturdays. A sharper version of the question: when the same production plays Friday, Saturday, and Sunday, which day do people pick?
Neon stores each performance as a separate event (e.g. “Fun Home - Friday, February 3 at 7:30 pm”) with no field linking the nights of a run, so event names were manually mapped to productions (hh.analytics.productions), and a production’s performances are split into separate runs when more than 45 days apart. This section keeps only production runs that played all three of Friday, Saturday, and Sunday — mostly mainstage shows running one to four weekends.
Two other differences from the counts above: attendance here is true headcount (ticket attendees whose registration succeeded, not registrations — one registration averages about two people), and canceled or moved performances, open rehearsals, online productions, and weekday 10 am school shows are excluded.
Code
from hh.viz import CATEGORICAL
from hh.analytics.productions import production_performances, full_weekend_runs, time_slot

perf = production_performances(regs, minorcats=("theater",))
fw = full_weekend_runs(perf)

day_order = [d for d in WEEKDAY_ORDER if d in set(fw["weekday"])]
day = (
    fw.groupby("weekday")
    .agg(performances=("swept_event_id", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
    .reindex(day_order)
)
day["attendees per performance"] = day["attendees"] / day["performances"]
day["share of attendees"] = day["attendees"] / day["attendees"].sum()

n_runs = fw["production_run"].nunique()
yr_span = f"{fw['starts_on'].min():%Y}–{fw['starts_on'].max():%Y}"
sat_share = f"{day.loc['Saturday', 'share of attendees']:.0%}"
per_perf = {
    d: f"{day.loc[d, 'attendees per performance']:.0f}"
    for d in ("Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday")
}
Across the 34 qualifying production runs (2013–2026), 296 performances drew 10,704 attendees. Any weekday rows below Friday–Sunday are the rare midweek performances inside a qualifying run (Winter Carnival 2015 played two Thursdays):
Code
day.style.format(
    {
        "performances": "{:,}",
        "attendees": "{:,}",
        "attendees per performance": "{:.1f}",
        "share of attendees": "{:.1%}",
    }
)

Saturday brings in the most people (42% of attendees) — but only because most runs play Saturday twice (matinee + evening). Per performance the three days are essentially tied: Friday 37, Saturday 35, and Sunday 37 attendees per show.
How each production splits across the three days (runs with fewer than 30 Fri–Sun attendees are left off the chart but appear in the table below):
Code
wk = fw[fw["weekday"].isin(["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"])]
run_day = wk.groupby(["production_run", "weekday"], as_index=False)["attendees"].sum()
run_day["share"] = run_day["attendees"] / run_day.groupby("production_run")[
    "attendees"
].transform("sum")
order = fw.groupby("production_run")["starts_on"].min().sort_values().index.tolist()
run_totals = wk.groupby("production_run")["attendees"].sum()
charted = [r for r in order if run_totals[r] >= 30]

fig = px.bar(
    run_day[run_day["production_run"].isin(charted)],
    x="share",
    y="production_run",
    color="weekday",
    orientation="h",
    category_orders={
        "production_run": charted[::-1],
        "weekday": ["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"],
    },
    color_discrete_map={
        "Friday": CATEGORICAL[0],
        "Saturday": CATEGORICAL[1],
        "Sunday": CATEGORICAL[2],
    },
    custom_data=["attendees"],
)
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate="%{x:.0%} — %{customdata[0]:,} attendees<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>"
)
fig.update_layout(
    height=740,
    xaxis_tickformat=".0%",
    xaxis_title="Share of run's Fri–Sun attendees",
    yaxis_title="",
)
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 3.2: Share of each run’s Fri–Sun attendees by day — one bar per production run, oldest at top
The same runs in detail — attendees by day, with each run’s dates and performance count:
Code
by_day = (
    fw.pivot_table(
        index="production_run",
        columns="weekday",
        values="attendees",
        aggfunc="sum",
        fill_value=0,
    )
    .reindex(columns=day_order)
)
by_day["Total"] = by_day.sum(axis=1)
meta = fw.groupby("production_run").agg(
    first=("starts_on", "min"), last=("starts_on", "max"), performances=("swept_event_id", "count")
)
detail = meta.join(by_day).reindex(order)
detail["first"] = detail["first"].dt.strftime("%b %d, %Y")
detail["last"] = detail["last"].dt.strftime("%b %d, %Y")
detail.index.name = None
detail = detail.rename(columns={"first": "First", "last": "Last", "performances": "Perfs"})
detail.style.format("{:,}", subset=[c for c in detail.columns if c not in ("First", "Last")])

Saturday’s totals hide one more split: most runs play a 2 pm matinee and an evening show that day. Splitting Saturday performances by time slot:
Code
sat = fw[fw["weekday"] == "Saturday"].assign(slot=lambda d: d["event_name"].map(time_slot))
slot = (
    sat.groupby("slot")
    .agg(performances=("swept_event_id", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
    .reindex(["matinee", "evening"])
)
slot["attendees per performance"] = slot["attendees"] / slot["performances"]
mat_rate = f"{slot.loc['matinee', 'attendees per performance']:.0f}"
eve_rate = f"{slot.loc['evening', 'attendees per performance']:.0f}"
slot.style.format(
    {"performances": "{:,}", "attendees": "{:,}", "attendees per performance": "{:.1f}"}
)

matinee

Saturday evenings draw 39 attendees per show versus 29 for Saturday matinees — the weakest slot of the weekend. Sunday’s single 2 pm show (37 per show) draws as well as any slot, so the matinee habit is a Sunday habit, not a Saturday one.

title: 3  When people come
text:
3.1.1 Do opera and music runs behave the same way?
Music and opera get the same treatment: map event names to productions, keep runs that played all of Friday, Saturday, and Sunday. Almost all music events are one-off concerts (Music from Salem plays single afternoons, cabarets play one or two nights), so nearly everything that qualifies is opera — the summer festival stagings of 2013–2017, which also played real midweek (Wednesday/Thursday) performances — plus one 2015 musical folktale with a theater-style four-weekend run.


Code
mo_fw = full_weekend_runs(production_performances(regs, minorcats=("music", "opera")))

mo_day_order = [d for d in WEEKDAY_ORDER if d in set(mo_fw["weekday"])]
mo_day = (
    mo_fw.groupby("weekday")
    .agg(performances=("swept_event_id", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
    .reindex(mo_day_order)
)
mo_day["attendees per performance"] = mo_day["attendees"] / mo_day["performances"]
mo_day["share of attendees"] = mo_day["attendees"] / mo_day["attendees"].sum()
mo_rate = {d: f"{mo_day.loc[d, 'attendees per performance']:.0f}" for d in mo_day_order}
n_mo_runs = mo_fw["production_run"].nunique()


The 9 qualifying runs, chronologically:


Code
mo_by_day = mo_fw.pivot_table(
    index="production_run",
    columns="weekday",
    values="attendees",
    aggfunc="sum",
    fill_value=0,
).reindex(columns=mo_day_order)
mo_by_day["Total"] = mo_by_day.sum(axis=1)
mo_meta = mo_fw.groupby("production_run").agg(
    first=("starts_on", "min"), last=("starts_on", "max"), performances=("swept_event_id", "count")
)
mo_order = mo_meta["first"].sort_values().index.tolist()
mo_detail = mo_meta.join(mo_by_day).reindex(mo_order)
mo_detail["first"] = mo_detail["first"].dt.strftime("%b %d, %Y")
mo_detail["last"] = mo_detail["last"].dt.strftime("%b %d, %Y")
mo_detail.index.name = None
mo_detail = mo_detail.rename(columns={"first": "First", "last": "Last", "performances": "Perfs"})
mo_detail.style.format(
    "{:,}", subset=[c for c in mo_detail.columns if c not in ("First", "Last")]
)

Opera audiences didn’t privilege the weekend: the midweek opera nights drew the most people per performance (Thursday 76, versus 49 on Friday and 38 on Saturday, the weakest day per show — diluted, as in theater, by Saturday matinees). The sample is small (a few midweek performances, all in the 2013–2017 festival era), so treat the midweek numbers as suggestive rather than precise.
The per-performance numbers above pool 2013–2026, but the day ranking has not been stable — Sunday has become the strongest day in recent years. That story is in How things changed over time. (That trend analysis is theater-only: the qualifying opera and music runs all fall in 2013–2017, too narrow a window for an over-time comparison.)

title: 4  Donors, patrons, and class-takers
text:
How do giving and participation overlap? Each household is flagged is_donor (gave a SUCCEEDED individual gift), is_patron (registered for a performance), and is_class_taker (registered for a class).


Code
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px
from hh.io import read_parquet
from hh.viz import style, CATEGORICAL, money

hh = read_parquet("processed", "households_summary.parquet")
counts = (
    pd.Series(
        {
            "Donors": hh["is_donor"].sum(),
            "Patrons (performances)": hh["is_patron"].sum(),
            "Class-takers": hh["is_class_taker"].sum(),
            "Donor and class": (hh["is_donor"] & hh["is_class_taker"]).sum(),
            "Donor and patron": (hh["is_donor"] & hh["is_patron"]).sum(),
            "All three": (hh["is_donor"] & hh["is_patron"] & hh["is_class_taker"]).sum(),
        },
        name="households",
    )
    .reset_index()
    .rename(columns={"index": "group"})
)
counts.style.format({"households": "{:,}"})







 
group
households

Donors

Code
fig = px.bar(counts, x="group", y="households", color="group", color_discrete_sequence=CATEGORICAL)
fig.update_layout(showlegend=False, xaxis_title="", yaxis_title="Households")
fig.update_traces(hovertemplate="%{y:,.0f}<extra></extra>")
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 4.1: Households in each giving / participation group




Do more-engaged households also live closer? Giving by distance band among donor households:


Code
donors = hh[hh["is_donor"]]
t = donors.groupby("distance_band", observed=True).agg(
    households=("id", "count"),
    mean_gift=("total_donations", "mean"),
    median_gift=("total_donations", "median"),
    total=("total_donations", "sum"),
)
t.style.format(
    {"households": "{:,}", "mean_gift": money, "median_gift": money, "total": money}
)







 
households
mean_gift
median_gift
total


distance_band
 
 
 
 




[0, 1)
162
$724
$100

title: 5  How things changed over time
text:
5.1 Fri vs Sat vs Sun within theater runs, over time
Two simple views across roughly five-year periods (2013–2015, 2016–2020, 2021–2026): where donor dollars come from, and which days draw attendance. Both are shown as shares within each period so changes in the mix are visible regardless of overall growth.
The figure above pools every event, so it mostly reflects what gets scheduled — more Saturday events means more Saturday attendance. To ask whether the audience’s day preference has shifted, this section holds the product constant: only theater production runs that played all three of Friday, Saturday, and Sunday (reconstructed by the manual event-name mapping described in When people come), measured as attendees per performance so days with two shows don’t get double credit. Headcount is ticket attendees whose registration succeeded.
Code
from hh.analytics.productions import production_performances, full_weekend_runs

fw = full_weekend_runs(
    production_performances(read_parquet("processed", "registrations_enriched.parquet"))
)
wk = fw[fw["weekday"].isin(["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"])].copy()
DAY_COLORS = {"Friday": CATEGORICAL[0], "Saturday": CATEGORICAL[1], "Sunday": CATEGORICAL[2]}

run_day = (
    wk.groupby(["production_run", "weekday"], as_index=False)
    .agg(performances=("starts_on", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
)
run_day["rate"] = run_day["attendees"] / run_day["performances"]
run_day["run_start"] = run_day["production_run"].map(
    wk.groupby("production_run")["starts_on"].min()
)
run_totals = wk.groupby("production_run")["attendees"].sum()
run_day = run_day[run_day["production_run"].map(run_totals) >= 30]

recent = run_day[run_day["run_start"] >= "2021-01-01"]
best_day = recent.loc[recent.groupby("production_run")["rate"].idxmax(), "weekday"]
n_recent = recent["production_run"].nunique()
n_recent_sun = int((best_day == "Sunday").sum())

wk["period"] = period(wk["starts_on"].dt.year)
pd_rate = (
    wk.groupby(["period", "weekday"], observed=True)
    .agg(performances=("starts_on", "count"), attendees=("attendees", "sum"))
    .reset_index()
)
pd_rate["rate"] = pd_rate["attendees"] / pd_rate["performances"]
rate_of = lambda p, d: pd_rate.set_index(["period", "weekday"])["rate"].loc[(p, d)]
One dot per run and day — each run’s attendees per performance on Fridays, Saturdays, and Sundays (runs with fewer than 30 Fri–Sun attendees omitted):
Code
fig = px.scatter(
    run_day.sort_values("run_start"),
    x="run_start",
    y="rate",
    color="weekday",
    color_discrete_map=DAY_COLORS,
    category_orders={"weekday": ["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"]},
    hover_name="production_run",
    custom_data=["performances", "attendees"],
)
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate=(
        "<b>%{hovertext}</b><br>%{y:.0f} attendees/perf "
        "(%{customdata[1]:,} over %{customdata[0]} shows)<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>"
    )
)
for d in ("Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"):  # 5-run rolling average as a guide line
    s = run_day[run_day["weekday"] == d].sort_values("run_start")
    fig.add_scatter(
        x=s["run_start"],
        y=s["rate"].rolling(5, center=True, min_periods=3).mean(),
        mode="lines",
        line=dict(color=DAY_COLORS[d], width=1.5),
        opacity=0.6,
        showlegend=False,
        hoverinfo="skip",
    )
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Run opening date", yaxis_title="Attendees per performance", legend_title=""
)
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 5.3: Attendees per performance by day, one point per full-weekend theater run
Aggregated to the same periods as the figures above:
Code
fig = px.bar(
    pd_rate,
    x="period",
    y="rate",
    color="weekday",
    barmode="group",
    color_discrete_map=DAY_COLORS,
    category_orders={"period": PERIODS, "weekday": ["Friday", "Saturday", "Sunday"]},
    custom_data=["performances"],
)
fig.update_traces(
    hovertemplate="%{y:.1f} attendees/perf (%{customdata[0]} shows)<extra>%{fullData.name}</extra>"
)
fig.update_layout(
    xaxis_title="Period", yaxis_title="Attendees per performance", legend_title=""
)
style(fig).show()




                            
                                            


Figure 5.4: Attendees per performance by day and period, full-weekend theater runs only
Per-performance attendance has roughly doubled across the periods, and the day ranking has flipped. In 2013–2015 Friday was the strongest day (27 per show vs 24 on Sunday); in 2016–2020 the days were about even; since 2021 Sunday is clearly strongest — 66 per show vs about 50 on Friday and Saturday. Sunday has been the best-attended day in 9 of the 9 full-weekend runs since 2021.
Recent-period caveats: 2021–2026 rests on 9 runs (52 performances), and recent runs rarely schedule Saturday matinees, which used to pull Saturday’s average down — yet Sunday leads anyway.
crumbs: 5  How things changed over time
